2: In livestock production, management of animal manure leads to major losses of nitrogen (N), phosphorus (P), and carbon (C). Manure management includes collection inside the housing system, storage (in- side and outside), processing, and field application. In the European Union (EU), about 149 Mtons of liquid pig manure, 448 Mtons of liquid cattle manure and 295 Mtons of solid cattle manure are produced

(Henning Lyngso et al., 2011), of which the Netherlands contributes considerably with 7% of the liquid pig manure, 10% of the liquid cattle manure. The production of solid manure is low with 0.04% of the EU production (CBS, 2011). Production of solid cattle manure in the Netherlands is currently increasing, as a result of initiatives to im- prove animal welfare. In the EU, only about half of the nitrogen (N) and 70% of the P excreted by animals is recycled as crop nutrient (Bouwman et al., 2009; Oenema et al., 2007), the rest is lost to the en- vironment causing adverse environmental impacts, such as climate change, terrestrial acidification, and marine eutrophication. To reduce environmental impacts, European directives, such as the Nitrates Direc- tive (91/676/EEC), the National Emission Ceilings (NEC) Directive
(2001/81/EC) and the Water framework Directive (200/60/EC) were implemented in order to reduce emissions from all Member States (EC, 2012; EU, 2013).

To reduce losses of P, and C from manure management and, there- fore, improve the efficiency of using N and P from manure, different strategies have been proposed (Burton and Turner, 2003; Sommer and Hutchings, 2001). Most strategies, however, focus on a single aspect of the manure management system, such as reducing ammonia (NH3) emission from outside manure storage by covering, or reducing NH3 emission from manure application by injection instead of broadcast spreading. Such single-issue strategies often cause reduction of one type of emission while increasing another type of emission, a phenomenon referred to as 'pollution swapping' We formulated strategies for integrated manure management of pig and dairy cattle manure to reduce environmental impact throughout the manure management system by using a structured design approach (De Vries et al., 2015). These strategies aim to reduce emissions of N, P and C and the use of fossil energy along the entire manure management system, or in other words, prevent pollution swapping. To validate this approach, we perform a quantitative assessment of the potential of these strategies to reduce the environmental impact. Life cycle assessment is (LCA) is a generally accepted method to quantify the environmental impact along the life cycle of a product (ISO-14040, 2006). The aim of this study was to assess the potential of the newly designed strategies for integrated pig and dairy cattle manure manage- ment, as designed by De Vries et al. (2015), to reduce environmental impact. We quantified the environmental impact, the N use efficiency (NUE), and P over application rate (as phosphorus pentoxide, P2O5) along the manure management system and demonstrated the potential to reduce environmental impact for the case of North Western Europe and the Netherlands.

In the European Union (EU), about 149 Mtons of liquid pig manure, 448 Mtons of liquid cattle manure and 295 Mtons of solid cattle manure are produced

(Henning Lyngso et al., 2011), of which the Netherlands contributes considerably with 7% of the liquid pig manure, 10% of the liquid cattle manure. To reduce environmental impacts, European directives, such as the Nitrates Direc- tive (91/676/EEC), the National Emission Ceilings (NEC) Directive
(2001/81/EC) and the Water framework Directive (200/60/EC) were implemented in order to reduce emissions from all Member States (EC, 2012; EU, 2013).

To reduce losses of P, and C from manure management and, there- fore, improve the efficiency of using N and P from manure, different strategies have been proposed (Burton and Turner, 2003; Sommer and Hutchings, 2001). Most strategies, however, focus on a single aspect of the manure management system, such as reducing ammonia (NH3) emission from outside manure storage by covering, or reducing NH3 emission from manure application by injection instead of broadcast spreading. Such single-issue strategies often cause reduction of one type of emission while increasing another type of emission, a phenomenon referred to as 'pollution swapping' We formulated strategies for integrated manure management of pig and dairy cattle manure to reduce environmental impact throughout the manure management system by using a structured design approach (De Vries et al., 2015). These strategies aim to reduce emissions of N, P and C and the use of fossil energy along the entire manure management system, or in other words, prevent pollution swapping. To validate this approach, we perform a quantitative assessment of the potential of these strategies to reduce the environmental impact. Life cycle assessment is (LCA) is a generally accepted method to quantify the environmental impact along the life cycle of a product (ISO-14040, 2006). The aim of this study was to assess the potential of the newly designed strategies for integrated pig and dairy cattle manure manage- ment, as designed by De Vries et al. (2015), to reduce environmental impact. We quantified the environmental impact, the N use efficiency (NUE), and P over application rate (as phosphorus pentoxide, P2O5) along the manure management system and demonstrated the potential to reduce environmental impact for the case of North Western Europe and the Netherlands.
5: In this study, we considered the changes in environmental impact of the strategies, or in other words, performed a consequential LCA (Finnveden et al., 2009). We, therefore, included all environmental impacts from processes that were affected by changes in the manure management system (Weidema et al., 2009).
6: The manure management system included the manure storage in the animal house, outside manure storage, manure processing, trans- port, and field application of manure, soil tillage and in-field traffic, and crop uptake of N until harvest. External processes included produc- tion of mineral fertilizer and production of electricity, heat, and fuel (Fig. 1). Avoided mineral fertilizer production was included, because the nutrients in the manure products (N, and potassium (K)) were considered to substitute nutrients from mineral N, P, and K fertilizers. Similarly, electricity and heat production were avoided with production of bio-energy. Animal production, crop management and transport were outside the system boundary, as they were assumed not to be af- fected by manure management strategies. Furthermore, emission from transport of manure was not considered, as we assumed the same dis- tances to apply for all situations. Emissions associated with the produc- tion of capital goods, such as the installations for manure processing, were excluded from the calculations.
7: The main function of the manure management systems compared was to manage livestock excreta from the moment of excretion until field application as fertilizer. We, therefore, used a common unit of 1 t excreted urine and feces, either mixed in liquid manure, or kept separate. The same chemical composition of excreta ensured that the same amount of nutrients and dry matter entered each management system.
8: <header>2.4. Definition of the NW EU reference, Dutch situation and strategies </header>

We applied the strategies for integrated manure management (IS) to current North West European (NW EU) practice (Ref) and used the Dutch situation to represent current progressive manure management (NL). NW EU represents intensive livestock and manure production. In Ref and NL, liquid cattle manure was applied to grassland and arable land for production of silage maize, whereas liquid pig manure was used for wheat production. Solid dairy cattle manure was applied to grassland.

In Ref, liquid pig and cattle manure were produced in a housing system with slatted floors. Manure was stored in-house for an average period of 5 months and in an outside storage tank without cover for an average period of 1 month (Table 1) (Burton and Turner, 2003;

X = no action; * numbers in cells indicate the number of times that the respective fertilizer product is applied at the specific time of the growing season. Storage methods: inside = inside the animal house, outside-r = outside roofed, outside-c = outside covered, outside-s = outside sealed. Application timing: gs = during growing season, pgs = prior to growing season/planting, egs = early growing season/just after planting.

De Vries et al., 2012a). Subsequently, pig and cattle manure were ap- plied to the field by broadcast spreading, without incorporation into the soil within 24 h. Application to arable land occurred only once in spring and twice to grassland during the growing season (Menzi et al., 1998). Liquid manure processing did not occur in Ref. Solid cattle ma- nure in Ref was produced in tied stalls. After in-house storage for a few days, it was removed to an outside storage, where it was stock piled uncovered for an average period of 4 months and spread in spring using a solid manure spreader (Menzi et al., 1998). For tillage and field traffic, inversion tillage by plowing arable land was assumed, whereas random traffic on arable and grassland for all cropping systems was assumed.

In NL, liquid pig manure was produced in a housing system with partly slatted floors (60%). Pig manure was stored in-house for an aver- age period of 5 months and in a covered outside storage tank for an average period of 1 month (Burton and Turner, 2003) and applied to arable land by deep injection (Van Bruggen et al., 2011). Application OC- curred once at the start of the growing season. Liquid cattle manure in NL was produced in a cubicle house with slatted floors and stored in- house for an average period of 4 months (Table 1). Subsequently, it was applied to grassland by shallow injection or to arable land by deep injection (Van Bruggen et al., 2011). Application on grassland OC- curred once prior to the growing season and three times during the growing season and to arable land once at the start of the growing sea- son (CBGV, 2012). Solid cattle manure in NL was produced in tied stalls. After in-house storage for a few days, the manure was stockpiled and stored outside under a roof for an average period of 4 months. Applica- tion occurred by a solid manure spreader once prior to the growing sea- son in spring (Ellen et al., 2007). For tillage and field traffic, inversion tillage of arable land and random traffic on arable and grassland was assumed for all systems.

IS were designed and described by De Vries et al. (submitted for publication), and summarized in Table 1. Strategies included the separation of urine and feces for pigs and dairy cattle in the housing system, called segregation. Pig and dairy cattle urine were acidified, and feces were digested and kept in sealed storages and applied by deep injection. Solid cattle manure was stored and mixed with zeolite and applied by broadcasting combined with rainfall or irrigation.

Solid cattle manure was stored and mixed with zeolite and applied by broadcasting combined with rainfall or irrigation.

De Vries et al., 2012a). Subsequently, pig and cattle manure were ap- plied to the field by broadcast spreading, without incorporation into the soil within 24 h. Application to arable land occurred only once in spring and twice to grassland during the growing season (Menzi et al., 1998). Liquid manure processing did not occur in Ref. Solid cattle ma- nure in Ref was produced in tied stalls. After in-house storage for a few days, it was removed to an outside storage, where it was stock piled uncovered for an average period of 4 months and spread in spring using a solid manure spreader (Menzi et al., 1998). For tillage and field traffic, inversion tillage by plowing arable land was assumed, whereas random traffic on arable and grassland for all cropping systems was assumed.

In NL, liquid pig manure was produced in a housing system with partly slatted floors (60%). Pig manure was stored in-house for an aver- age period of 5 months and in a covered outside storage tank for an average period of 1 month (Burton and Turner, 2003) and applied to arable land by deep injection (Van Bruggen et al., 2011). Application OC- curred once at the start of the growing season. Liquid cattle manure in NL was produced in a cubicle house with slatted floors and stored in- house for an average period of 4 months (Table 1). Subsequently, it was applied to grassland by shallow injection or to arable land by deep injection (Van Bruggen et al., 2011). Application on grassland OC- curred once prior to the growing season and three times during the growing season and to arable land once at the start of the growing sea- son (CBGV, 2012). Solid cattle manure in NL was produced in tied stalls. After in-house storage for a few days, the manure was stockpiled and stored outside under a roof for an average period of 4 months. Applica- tion occurred by a solid manure spreader once prior to the growing sea- son in spring (Ellen et al., 2007). For tillage and field traffic, inversion tillage of arable land and random traffic on arable and grassland was assumed for all systems.

IS were designed and described by De Vries et al. (submitted for publication), and summarized in Table 1. Strategies included the separation of urine and feces for pigs and dairy cattle in the housing system, called segregation. Pig and dairy cattle urine were acidified, and feces were digested and kept in sealed storages and applied by deep injection. Solid cattle manure was stored and mixed with zeolite and applied by broadcasting combined with rainfall or irrigation.

14: The chemical compositions of the manure products in Ref and NL were based on Menzi (2002) (Table 2). To calculate the compositions after excretion, values in Table 2 were corrected for storage emissions under NW EU conditions using a mass balance approach. The composi- tions of the urine and feces used in IS were based on De Haan et al. (2003) for cattle and on Aarnink et al. (2007) for pigs (Table 3). Because the compositions in IS and Ref were taken from different literature sources, we scaled the urine and feces compositions in IS to the liquid manure composition in Ref, based on the method used in De Vries et al. (2013) (Supplementary information section 1). This ensured that the same amount of nutrients and dry matter entered IS and Ref in ac- cordance with the unit for comparison. Following a mass balance, the compositions of all products (Tables 2 and 3) were calculated for each stage in the manure management system using emission factors in Table 4.
15: <header>2.5.2. Data for in-house management and manure storage </header>

During in-house and outside storage of liquid and solid manure, and of urine and feces, emissions of NH3, N20, NO, N, and CH4 were consid- ered. Emission factors in Ref and NL were based on IPCC and EMEP Corinair guidelines (EMEP, 2009; IPCC, 2006b) (Table 4). Emission factors for NH3 in NL were based on the national inventory data (Groenestein et al., 2012a; Van Bruggen et al., 2011). Emission factors for outside storage in IS were calculated based on the reduction percent- ages in Table 6. We assumed that sealing storages reduced emissions of NH3, CH4, and N20 by 90% (Table 6). Emission of NH3 for in-house management of solid cattle manure was reduced by adding zeolite (50 ton manure) in IS (Shah et al., 2012a). Indirect emissions of N20 from nitrification of NH3, NOx, and NO3 in the environment were included; 1% of NH3-N + NOx-N (95% confidence interval (CI) of 0.2-5%) and 0.75% of NO3-N (95% CI of 0.05-2.5%) (IPCC, 2006b).
We assumed that 2% of the N present in solid cattle manure leaches as NO3 during outside storage (Table 3) (EMEP, 2009).

Emission of CH4 from storage in Ref and NL were based on data from the Rains model (Klimont and Brink, 2004). Addition of zeolite slightly increased CH4 emission from solid cattle manure storage (Shah, 2012).

21: <header>2.5.3. Data for digestion, acidification and zeolite addition </header>

In IS, cattle and pig feces were anaerobically digested to reduce CH4 emission and fossil fuel use by production of bio-energy from biogas, a mixture of CH4 and CO2. We assumed a continuous stirred tank reactor (CSTR) operating at mesophilic temperature (ca. 35 °C) with a hydraulic retention time of 60 days (De Vries et al., 2015). Respective CH4 yields for anaerobic digestion of segregated cattle and pig feces were assumed to be 25 and 37.5 m³ CH4 t fresh matter, respectively (Timmerman et al., 2009; Van Dooren, 2010 Unpublished data). The biogas was used in a combined heat and power unit (CHP) to generate electricity

and heat. The energetic efficiency of the CHP was 80% and the electric ef- ficiency was 35%. Produced electricity was assumed to avoid electricity on the electricity grid, whereas 50% of the remaining heat after use for the digestion process was assumed to avoid fossil-based heat (De Vries et al., 2012b). The energy required for anaerobic digestion was 66 MJ electricity per ton of substrate and 166 MJ heat per ton of sub- strate (Berglund and Börjesson, 2006).
During anaerobic digestion, 1.5% of the CH4 produced was assumed to emit from the installation (1%) and the CHP (0.5%) as leakage (De Vries et al., 2012a).

22: <header>2.5.3. Data for digestion, acidification and zeolite addition </header>

In IS, cattle and pig feces were anaerobically digested to reduce CH4 emission and fossil fuel use by production of bio-energy from biogas, a mixture of CH4 and CO2. We assumed a continuous stirred tank reactor (CSTR) operating at mesophilic temperature (ca. 35 °C) with a hydraulic retention time of 60 days (De Vries et al., 2015). Respective CH4 yields for anaerobic digestion of segregated cattle and pig feces were assumed to be 25 and 37.5 m³ CH4 t fresh matter, respectively (Timmerman et al., 2009; Van Dooren, 2010 Unpublished data). The biogas was used in a combined heat and power unit (CHP) to generate electricity

and heat. The energetic efficiency of the CHP was 80% and the electric ef- ficiency was 35%. Produced electricity was assumed to avoid electricity on the electricity grid, whereas 50% of the remaining heat after use for the digestion process was assumed to avoid fossil-based heat (De Vries et al., 2012b). The energy required for anaerobic digestion was 66 MJ electricity per ton of substrate and 166 MJ heat per ton of sub- strate (Berglund and Börjesson, 2006).
During anaerobic digestion, 1.5% of the CH4 produced was assumed to emit from the installation (1%) and the CHP (0.5%) as leakage (De Vries et al., 2012a).

24: <header>2.5.3. Data for digestion, acidification and zeolite addition </header>

In IS, cattle and pig feces were anaerobically digested to reduce CH4 emission and fossil fuel use by production of bio-energy from biogas, a mixture of CH4 and CO2. We assumed a continuous stirred tank reactor (CSTR) operating at mesophilic temperature (ca. 35 °C) with a hydraulic retention time of 60 days (De Vries et al., 2015). Respective CH4 yields for anaerobic digestion of segregated cattle and pig feces were assumed to be 25 and 37.5 m³ CH4 t fresh matter, respectively (Timmerman et al., 2009; Van Dooren, 2010 Unpublished data). The biogas was used in a combined heat and power unit (CHP) to generate electricity

and heat. The energetic efficiency of the CHP was 80% and the electric ef- ficiency was 35%. Produced electricity was assumed to avoid electricity on the electricity grid, whereas 50% of the remaining heat after use for the digestion process was assumed to avoid fossil-based heat (De Vries et al., 2012b). The energy required for anaerobic digestion was 66 MJ electricity per ton of substrate and 166 MJ heat per ton of sub- strate (Berglund and Börjesson, 2006).
During anaerobic digestion, 1.5% of the CH4 produced was assumed to emit from the installation (1%) and the CHP (0.5%) as leakage (De Vries et al., 2012a).

In IS, urine was acidified during storage and digested feces were acidified just before application with sulfuric acid (H2SO4). Production impact of H2SO4 was excluded as this was negligible for the end result
(Wesnaes et al., 2009). Environmental impact from producing zeolite, added to solid cattle manure, was assumed equal to the impact of stone meal production (EcoinventCentre, 2007).

26: <header>2.5.3. Data for digestion, acidification and zeolite addition </header>

In IS, cattle and pig feces were anaerobically digested to reduce CH4 emission and fossil fuel use by production of bio-energy from biogas, a mixture of CH4 and CO2. We assumed a continuous stirred tank reactor (CSTR) operating at mesophilic temperature (ca. 35 °C) with a hydraulic retention time of 60 days (De Vries et al., 2015). Respective CH4 yields for anaerobic digestion of segregated cattle and pig feces were assumed to be 25 and 37.5 m³ CH4 t fresh matter, respectively (Timmerman et al., 2009; Van Dooren, 2010 Unpublished data). The biogas was used in a combined heat and power unit (CHP) to generate electricity

and heat. The energetic efficiency of the CHP was 80% and the electric ef- ficiency was 35%. Produced electricity was assumed to avoid electricity on the electricity grid, whereas 50% of the remaining heat after use for the digestion process was assumed to avoid fossil-based heat (De Vries et al., 2012b). The energy required for anaerobic digestion was 66 MJ electricity per ton of substrate and 166 MJ heat per ton of sub- strate (Berglund and Börjesson, 2006).
During anaerobic digestion, 1.5% of the CH4 produced was assumed to emit from the installation (1%) and the CHP (0.5%) as leakage (De Vries et al., 2012a).

28: <header>2.5.3. Data for digestion, acidification and zeolite addition </header>

In IS, cattle and pig feces were anaerobically digested to reduce CH4 emission and fossil fuel use by production of bio-energy from biogas, a mixture of CH4 and CO2. We assumed a continuous stirred tank reactor (CSTR) operating at mesophilic temperature (ca. 35 °C) with a hydraulic retention time of 60 days (De Vries et al., 2015). Respective CH4 yields for anaerobic digestion of segregated cattle and pig feces were assumed to be 25 and 37.5 m³ CH4 t fresh matter, respectively (Timmerman et al., 2009; Van Dooren, 2010 Unpublished data). The biogas was used in a combined heat and power unit (CHP) to generate electricity

and heat. The energetic efficiency of the CHP was 80% and the electric ef- ficiency was 35%. Produced electricity was assumed to avoid electricity on the electricity grid, whereas 50% of the remaining heat after use for the digestion process was assumed to avoid fossil-based heat (De Vries et al., 2012b). The energy required for anaerobic digestion was 66 MJ electricity per ton of substrate and 166 MJ heat per ton of sub- strate (Berglund and Börjesson, 2006).
During anaerobic digestion, 1.5% of the CH4 produced was assumed to emit from the installation (1%) and the CHP (0.5%) as leakage (De Vries et al., 2012a).

30: <header>2.5.3. Data for digestion, acidification and zeolite addition </header>

In IS, cattle and pig feces were anaerobically digested to reduce CH4 emission and fossil fuel use by production of bio-energy from biogas, a mixture of CH4 and CO2. We assumed a continuous stirred tank reactor (CSTR) operating at mesophilic temperature (ca. 35 °C) with a hydraulic retention time of 60 days (De Vries et al., 2015). Respective CH4 yields for anaerobic digestion of segregated cattle and pig feces were assumed to be 25 and 37.5 m³ CH4 t fresh matter, respectively (Timmerman et al., 2009; Van Dooren, 2010 Unpublished data). The biogas was used in a combined heat and power unit (CHP) to generate electricity

and heat. The energetic efficiency of the CHP was 80% and the electric ef- ficiency was 35%. Produced electricity was assumed to avoid electricity on the electricity grid, whereas 50% of the remaining heat after use for the digestion process was assumed to avoid fossil-based heat (De Vries et al., 2012b). The energy required for anaerobic digestion was 66 MJ electricity per ton of substrate and 166 MJ heat per ton of sub- strate (Berglund and Börjesson, 2006).
During anaerobic digestion, 1.5% of the CH4 produced was assumed to emit from the installation (1%) and the CHP (0.5%) as leakage (De Vries et al., 2012a).

33: <header>2.5.4. Data for field application and soil tillage </header>

During and after field application of liquid manure, solid cattle manure, urine and feces, emissions of N20, NH3, NO, N, and NO3 were considered. Changes in CH4 emission from field traffic were excluded, as the contribution of CH4 emission from the field to overall impact of climate change from manure management is negligible (Vermeulen and Mosquera, 2009). Emissions in Ref and NL were based on IPCC and EMEP Corinair guidelines, whereas NH3 emissions for NL were based on specific national values (Table 5). Emission factors of IS were based on Ref values and reductions in Table 6. In the situation of combined reductions, a multiplication of reduction factors was ap- plied. For example, the N20 emission after application of digested cattle feces were reduced due to anaerobic digestion (less available C) and CTF (increased soil aeration) as follows: 1% (1-44%) X (1-35%) (Table 6). Similarly, this was done for N20 emission reduction for storage of solid cattle manure, and NH3 emission reduction and the combined effect of injection and acidification and for combined zeolite addition and irriga- tion in case of solid cattle manure.

Leaching and run-off of NO3 in Ref and IS was computed as a frac- tion of the applied N (IPCC, 2006b) (Table 5). The applied N was corrected for NH3 and N20 emission prior to multiplying with the leaching fraction (Dekker et al., 2009; Langevin et al., 2010). The leaching fraction for grassland was assumed 0.36 of the fraction on ara- ble land (Schröder et al., 2007). The N uptake by each crop was comput- ed using the apparent N recovery (ANR) of that respective crop. The ANR of grass was 75% (range of 60-90%), of wheat 70% (range of 60- 80%), and that of maize 50% (range of 40-60%) (Greenwood et al., 1989; Schröder et al., 1998; Ten Berge et al., 2007). The ranges were assumed to be the 95% Cls. We included similar ANRs in IS and in Ref, implying that when more N was available from manure products, more mineral N fertilizer was avoided, meaning an environmental gain.

The amount of mineral N fertilizer avoided by using the N from ma- nure products was calculated by using the N fertilizer replacement value (NFRV). The NFRV of the Nmin fraction in the manure products was calculated by subtracting the NH3 emission from the Nmin fraction. NFRV of the Norg fraction in the manure products was calculated by mul- tiplication with the percentages given in Table 5. In the IS we included

an increase of the NFRV as a result of improved synchronization of N application and crop N uptake (Table 6). The increase for grassland was assumed 5% and for arable land 7.5%. The fertilizer replacement values for P and K were assumed to be 100%.

In IS, energy use was reduced by 42% with non-inversion tillage (Table 6). Energy reduction from controlled field traffic was assumed to be counteracted by increased trafficking from applying urine and feces more often during the growing season.

2.5.5. Data for external processes and environmental impact assessment Emissions and environmental impacts from external processes (Fig. 1) were based on the Ecoinvent database (EcoinventCentre, 2007). Mineral fertilizer used was assumed to be calcium ammonium nitrate for N, triple super phosphate for P, and potassium chloride for K (De Vries et al., 2012a). The production mix for electricity and heat was based on the Dutch situation; 28% coal-based, 67% natural gas- based, and 5% wind-based electricity; and 79% natural gas-based and 21% light fuel oil-based heat (De Vries et al., 2012a).

The environmental impact assessment was conducted based on the ReCiPe v.1.04 midpoint impact assessment method (Goedkoop et al., 2009). We considered the following impact categories: climate change (CC) in kg CO2-eq, including: CO2, CH4, and N20. Biogenic C emission was excluded from the calculation as it represents C that is taken up by crops earlier (IPCC, 1997). Similarly, C-sequestration was excluded due to its small contribution; terrestrial acidification (TA) in kg SO2- eq, including: NH3, NOx, and SO2; marine eutrophication (ME) in kg N-eq, including: NH3, NOx, and NO particulate matter formation (PMF) in kg PM10-eq, including: NH3, NOx, and SO2 as precursors of par- ticulate matter; and fossil fuel depletion (FFD), in kg oil-eq. We calculat- ed NUE as the ratio of N taken up by the crop to the N excreted by the animal, and the P2O5 over application rate (POA). The POA represents the amount of P2O5 over applied relative to the demanded N, i.e. representing the fittingness of the manure product to the demand of the crop and the addition to the soil P surplus. We computed this by comparing the N/P2O5 ratio of the manure product with the N/P2O5 ratio demanded by the crop. The N/P2O5 ratio demand was 3.7 for cut grassland, 3.3 for maize, and 2.6 for wheat (Schröder, 2005).

Similarly, C-sequestration was excluded due to its small contribution; terrestrial acidification (TA) in kg SO2- eq, including: NH3, NOx, and SO2; marine eutrophication (ME) in kg N-eq, including: NH3, NOx, and NO particulate matter formation (PMF) in kg PM10-eq, including: NH3, NOx, and SO2 as precursors of par- ticulate matter; and fossil fuel depletion (FFD), in kg oil-eq. We calculat- ed NUE as the ratio of N taken up by the crop to the N excreted by the animal, and the P2O5 over application rate (POA). The POA represents the amount of P2O5 over applied relative to the demanded N, i.e. representing the fittingness of the manure product to the demand of the crop and the addition to the soil P surplus. We computed this by comparing the N/P2O5 ratio of the manure product with the N/P2O5 ratio demanded by the crop. The N/P2O5 ratio demand was 3.7 for cut grassland, 3.3 for maize, and 2.6 for wheat (Schröder, 2005).
37: <header>2.5.4. Data for field application and soil tillage </header>

During and after field application of liquid manure, solid cattle manure, urine and feces, emissions of N20, NH3, NO, N, and NO3 were considered. Changes in CH4 emission from field traffic were excluded, as the contribution of CH4 emission from the field to overall impact of climate change from manure management is negligible (Vermeulen and Mosquera, 2009). Emissions in Ref and NL were based on IPCC and EMEP Corinair guidelines, whereas NH3 emissions for NL were based on specific national values (Table 5). Emission factors of IS were based on Ref values and reductions in Table 6. In the situation of combined reductions, a multiplication of reduction factors was ap- plied. For example, the N20 emission after application of digested cattle feces were reduced due to anaerobic digestion (less available C) and CTF (increased soil aeration) as follows: 1% (1-44%) X (1-35%) (Table 6). Similarly, this was done for N20 emission reduction for storage of solid cattle manure, and NH3 emission reduction and the combined effect of injection and acidification and for combined zeolite addition and irriga- tion in case of solid cattle manure.

Leaching and run-off of NO3 in Ref and IS was computed as a frac- tion of the applied N (IPCC, 2006b) (Table 5). The applied N was corrected for NH3 and N20 emission prior to multiplying with the leaching fraction (Dekker et al., 2009; Langevin et al., 2010). The leaching fraction for grassland was assumed 0.36 of the fraction on ara- ble land (Schröder et al., 2007). The N uptake by each crop was comput- ed using the apparent N recovery (ANR) of that respective crop. The ANR of grass was 75% (range of 60-90%), of wheat 70% (range of 60- 80%), and that of maize 50% (range of 40-60%) (Greenwood et al., 1989; Schröder et al., 1998; Ten Berge et al., 2007). The ranges were assumed to be the 95% Cls. We included similar ANRs in IS and in Ref, implying that when more N was available from manure products, more mineral N fertilizer was avoided, meaning an environmental gain.

The amount of mineral N fertilizer avoided by using the N from ma- nure products was calculated by using the N fertilizer replacement value (NFRV). The NFRV of the Nmin fraction in the manure products was calculated by subtracting the NH3 emission from the Nmin fraction. NFRV of the Norg fraction in the manure products was calculated by mul- tiplication with the percentages given in Table 5. In the IS we included

an increase of the NFRV as a result of improved synchronization of N application and crop N uptake (Table 6). The increase for grassland was assumed 5% and for arable land 7.5%. The fertilizer replacement values for P and K were assumed to be 100%.

In IS, energy use was reduced by 42% with non-inversion tillage (Table 6). Energy reduction from controlled field traffic was assumed to be counteracted by increased trafficking from applying urine and feces more often during the growing season.

2.5.5. Data for external processes and environmental impact assessment Emissions and environmental impacts from external processes (Fig. 1) were based on the Ecoinvent database (EcoinventCentre, 2007). Mineral fertilizer used was assumed to be calcium ammonium nitrate for N, triple super phosphate for P, and potassium chloride for K (De Vries et al., 2012a). The production mix for electricity and heat was based on the Dutch situation; 28% coal-based, 67% natural gas- based, and 5% wind-based electricity; and 79% natural gas-based and 21% light fuel oil-based heat (De Vries et al., 2012a).

The environmental impact assessment was conducted based on the ReCiPe v.1.04 midpoint impact assessment method (Goedkoop et al., 2009). We considered the following impact categories: climate change (CC) in kg CO2-eq, including: CO2, CH4, and N20. Biogenic C emission was excluded from the calculation as it represents C that is taken up by crops earlier (IPCC, 1997). Similarly, C-sequestration was excluded due to its small contribution; terrestrial acidification (TA) in kg SO2- eq, including: NH3, NOx, and SO2; marine eutrophication (ME) in kg N-eq, including: NH3, NOx, and NO particulate matter formation (PMF) in kg PM10-eq, including: NH3, NOx, and SO2 as precursors of par- ticulate matter; and fossil fuel depletion (FFD), in kg oil-eq. We calculat- ed NUE as the ratio of N taken up by the crop to the N excreted by the animal, and the P2O5 over application rate (POA). The POA represents the amount of P2O5 over applied relative to the demanded N, i.e. representing the fittingness of the manure product to the demand of the crop and the addition to the soil P surplus. We computed this by comparing the N/P2O5 ratio of the manure product with the N/P2O5 ratio demanded by the crop. The N/P2O5 ratio demand was 3.7 for cut grassland, 3.3 for maize, and 2.6 for wheat (Schröder, 2005).

40: A Monte Carlo uncertainty analysis was conducted to assess the ef- fect of variation in emission factors and crop-N uptake on final results and comparisons between Ref, NL and IS. We included the following parameters and defined their 95% CI and distribution functions: N20 (direct and indirect, and the related NO and N2 emissions), NH3, CH4, NO3, and the ANR (Tables 4 and 5). Uncertainty in the environmental impact of mineral fertilizer production was included as a result from varying N emissions. Uncertainty in electricity production was excluded.

In IS, emission factors were calculated by using the factors from Ref and additionally applying a 95% CI and distribution function to the reduction percentages (Table 6). The distribution function of N20 was assumed to be lognormal, the others were assumed to be normally dis- tributed (De Vries et al., 2013; Payraudeau et al., 2007). We ran 1000 Monte Carlo simulations to compute the variation in all final environ- mental impact categories, the NUE, and POA. Differences between Ref, NL, and IS were indicated for their significance with a two-sided t-test.
41: Applying IS reduced the CC impact in all manure-crop combinations (Fig. The net CC impact of IS ranged from - 45.8 to - 77.8 kg CO2-eq, whereas the net impact of Ref ranged from 71.6 to 417 kg CO2-eq. Differences between IS and Ref had three main causes: 1. segregation of urine and feces reduced CH4 emission from in-house management, without increasing other greenhouse gas emissions (7.02 to 58.7 81.5 to 417 kg CO2-eq for in-house management in Ref; Supplementary information, Tables SI 1 and 2 include all detailed emissions and impacts); 2. bio-energy production from feces reduced the production of fossil electricity and heat, and related GHG emissions, as shown in the outside storage/processing phase of the manure management sys- tem in Fig. 2 (132 MJ electricity and 77 MJ heat avoided with digested cattle feces and 180 MJ electricity and 110 MJ heat avoided with pig feces); and 3. reduced N emissions and improved management resulted in more avoided mineral N fertilizer application and production (-73.1 to - 102 VS. Ref 33.5 to - 50.6 kg CO2-eq). CC impact change from non-inversion tillage instead of conventional tillage was small; maxi- mum of 0.61 kg CO2-eq.

Applying IS reduced FFD in all manure-crop combinations (Fig. 2). The net FFD impact of IS ranged from - 371 to - 1050 MJ, whereas the net impact of Ref ranged from - 89 to - 185 MJ. Differences be- tween Ref and IS had two main causes: 1. reduced N emissions resulted in more avoided mineral N fertilizer application and production (343 to 499 VS. 188 to 287 MJ). 2. bio-energy production from the feces fraction reduced the production of fossil electricity and heat (508 to 710 MJ). FFD for outside storage and processing in the IS with solid cattle manure and grass slightly increased (38.0 from the production of zeolite. FFD change from applying non-inversion tillage in the IS, instead of con- ventional tillage in Ref, was small (at the most 18 MJ).

Applying IS reduced the TA impact in all manure product and crop combinations (Fig. The net TA impact of IS ranged from 0.71 to 2.30 kg SO2-eq, whereas the net impact of Ref ranged from 7.22 to 9.85 kg SO2-eq. The differences between Ref and IS had three main causes: 1. segregation of urine and feces reduced NH3 emission from housing (0.80 to 2.54 VS. 2.05 to 4.49 kg SO2-eq in the housing system of Ref); 2. sealed outside storages reduced the emission of NH3; and 3. application of urine and feces with acidification and injection reduced NH3 emission from the field (0.34 VS. 3.77 to 4.05 kg SO2-eq in Ref). TA reduction in IS with solid cattle manure was caused by reduced NH3 emission from zeolite addition during storage (0.44 kg SO2-eq for in-house and outside storage VS. 5.84 kg SO2-eq for the Ref).
Applying IS reduced the ME impact in all manure product and crop combinations (Fig. 2). The net ME impact of IS ranged from 0 to

0.18 kg N-eq, whereas the net impact of Ref ranged from 0.31 to 0.65 kg N-eq. The difference between Ref and IS was a result of reduced N emissions and improved management in the manure management system and consequently more avoided mineral N fertilizer application and leaching of NO3 (0.57 to 1.73 VS. 0.27 to 0.80 kg N-eq in the Ref from avoided fertilizer application).

Applying IS reduced the PMF impact in all manure product and crop combinations (Fig. 2). The net PMF impact of IS ranged from 0 to 0.25 kg PM10-eq, whereas the net impact of Ref ranged from 0.93 to 1.27 kg PM10-eq. Differences between Ref and IS were caused by similar factors as the ones that reduced TA, as NH3 was the main contributor to this impact category.

Applying IS increased NUE and reduced POA in all manure product and crop combinations (Fig. 2). The NUE of IS ranged from 44 to 70%, whereas the NUE of Ref ranged from 18 to 33%. POA in IS ranged from 0.10 to 0.55 kg P2O5 and in Ref from 0.77 to 1.56 kg P2O5. NUE in IS was more than doubled compared with Ref in all manure product and crop combinations. This increase for IS was mainly caused by reduced N emissions and improved management. The POA decreased in IS compared with Ref, also as a result of more retained N in the manure products, i.e. increasing the N/P2O5 ratio of the manure products.

The differences between Ref and IS had three main causes: 1. segregation of urine and feces reduced NH3 emission from housing (0.80 to 2.54 VS. 2.05 to 4.49 kg SO2-eq in the housing system of Ref); 2. sealed outside storages reduced the emission of NH3; and 3. application of urine and feces with acidification and injection reduced NH3 emission from the field (0.34 VS. 3.77 to 4.05 kg SO2-eq in Ref). TA reduction in IS with solid cattle manure was caused by reduced NH3 emission from zeolite addition during storage (0.44 kg SO2-eq for in-house and outside storage VS. 5.84 kg SO2-eq for the Ref).
Applying IS reduced the ME impact in all manure product and crop combinations (Fig. 2). The net ME impact of IS ranged from 0 to

0.18 kg N-eq, whereas the net impact of Ref ranged from 0.31 to 0.65 kg N-eq. The difference between Ref and IS was a result of reduced N emissions and improved management in the manure management system and consequently more avoided mineral N fertilizer application and leaching of NO3 (0.57 to 1.73 VS. 0.27 to 0.80 kg N-eq in the Ref from avoided fertilizer application).

Applying IS reduced the PMF impact in all manure product and crop combinations (Fig. 2). The net PMF impact of IS ranged from 0 to 0.25 kg PM10-eq, whereas the net impact of Ref ranged from 0.93 to 1.27 kg PM10-eq. Differences between Ref and IS were caused by similar factors as the ones that reduced TA, as NH3 was the main contributor to this impact category.

Applying IS increased NUE and reduced POA in all manure product and crop combinations (Fig. 2). The NUE of IS ranged from 44 to 70%, whereas the NUE of Ref ranged from 18 to 33%. POA in IS ranged from 0.10 to 0.55 kg P2O5 and in Ref from 0.77 to 1.56 kg P2O5. NUE in IS was more than doubled compared with Ref in all manure product and crop combinations. This increase for IS was mainly caused by reduced N emissions and improved management. The POA decreased in IS compared with Ref, also as a result of more retained N in the manure products, i.e. increasing the N/P2O5 ratio of the manure products.
46: Applying IS reduced the CC impact in all manure-crop combinations (Fig. The net CC impact of IS ranged from - 45.8 to - 77.8 kg CO2-eq, whereas the net impact of Ref ranged from 71.6 to 417 kg CO2-eq. Differences between IS and Ref had three main causes: 1. segregation of urine and feces reduced CH4 emission from in-house management, without increasing other greenhouse gas emissions (7.02 to 58.7 81.5 to 417 kg CO2-eq for in-house management in Ref; Supplementary information, Tables SI 1 and 2 include all detailed emissions and impacts); 2. bio-energy production from feces reduced the production of fossil electricity and heat, and related GHG emissions, as shown in the outside storage/processing phase of the manure management sys- tem in Fig. 2 (132 MJ electricity and 77 MJ heat avoided with digested cattle feces and 180 MJ electricity and 110 MJ heat avoided with pig feces); and 3. reduced N emissions and improved management resulted in more avoided mineral N fertilizer application and production (-73.1 to - 102 VS. Ref 33.5 to - 50.6 kg CO2-eq). CC impact change from non-inversion tillage instead of conventional tillage was small; maxi- mum of 0.61 kg CO2-eq.

47: NL had a net CC impact of 75.8 to 387 kg CO2-eq with the highest impact from liquid pig manure combined with wheat (Table SI2). CC impacts did not differ between NL and Ref as a result of using the same emission factors for CH4 and N20.

NL had a net FFD impact of to with the highest im- pact from solid cattle manure and grass (Table SI2). FFD impact for liq- uid pig and cattle manure was lower in NL than the Ref, mainly because more avoided impact from production of mineral N fertilizer (-298 to 330 MJ for NL VS. max. - 287 MJ in Ref). This difference between Ref and NL was due to less N emission, mainly NH3, after application in NL.

NL had a net TA impact of 0.72 to 8.94 kg SO2-eq, with the highest impact from solid cattle manure and grass (Table SI2). The lower impact for all other manure and crop combinations in NL was mainly due to lower NH3 emission during in-house and outside storage and field ap- plication. In NL, injection of liquid manure is obliged by either shallow or deep injection (0.19 to 1.78 kg SO2-eq in NL VS. 3.77 to 4.05 kg SO2- eq in Ref). In NL, covering of outside storages for liquid pig manure is obliged. This further reduces the risk of NH3 emission during storage compared with NW EU conditions where covering is not always obliga- tory. The TA impact for solid cattle manure and grass was similar in NL compared with Ref (8.94 VS. 8.75 kg SO2-eq), but higher emissions of NH3 occurred in NL during field application, whereas lower NH3 emis- sion occurred during in-house and outside storage.
NL had a net ME impact of 0.12 to 0.63 kg N-eq, with the highest impact for solid cattle manure and grass (Table SI2).

NL had a net PMF impact of 0.07 to 1.19 kg PM10-eq, with the highest impact for solid cattle manure and grass (Table SI2). The lower impact for all manure and crop combinations in NL compared with Ref was mainly due to lower NH3 emission as in the TA impact category.

NL had a NUE of 27 to 55% and a POA of 0.17 to 1.65 kg P2O5 (Table SI2). In NL, NUE was higher compared with Ref in most manure and crop combinations due to less N losses as indicated earlier. The POA of NL was lower compared with Ref also showing the lower N losses in the manure management system.

The coefficient of variation for CC in Ref (CV, i.e. the SD divided by the mean; Table SI 3) ranged from 25-44%. Uncertainty was highest with solid cattle manure and grass as a result of higher N20 emission that has a relative higher variation. The CV for CC in NL ranged from 27 to 48%. The CV for CC from applying IS ranged from 12 to 75% with the greatest uncertainty with pig urine, digested pig feces and wheat, as a result of higher CH4 emission.

Uncertainty in FFD was related mainly to the varying amount of avoided mineral fertilizer. Hence, variation for FFD was relatively small. The CV for FFD in Ref ranged from 5 to 26%. CVs for NL and IS ranged from 5 to 27% and 1 to 3%, respectively.

The CV for TA in Ref ranged from 19 to 24%. CV for NL ranged from 25 to 37% and of IS from 28 to 62%. In the IS uncertainty was higher as a re- sult of the additional variation in the reduction factors applied to calcu- late the emission factors (Section 2.5 and Table 6). The CV for PMF in IS ranged from 55 to 112% being larger than the CV of the Ref that ranged from 20 to 24%.

The CV for ME in Ref, IS, and NL were similar and ranged from 16 to 200%. The CVs for NUE and POA ranged from 10 to 30% and 3 to 331%, respectively. The CV for POA in IS had the greatest uncertainty with pig urine, digested pig feces and wheat resulting from greater variation in N emissions and small values.

The coefficient of variation for CC in Ref (CV, i.e. the SD divided by the mean; Table SI 3) ranged from 25-44%. Uncertainty was highest with solid cattle manure and grass as a result of higher N20 emission that has a relative higher variation. The CV for CC in NL ranged from 27 to 48%. The CV for CC from applying IS ranged from 12 to 75% with the greatest uncertainty with pig urine, digested pig feces and wheat, as a result of higher CH4 emission.

Uncertainty in FFD was related mainly to the varying amount of avoided mineral fertilizer. Hence, variation for FFD was relatively small. The CV for FFD in Ref ranged from 5 to 26%. CVs for NL and IS ranged from 5 to 27% and 1 to 3%, respectively.

The CV for TA in Ref ranged from 19 to 24%. CV for NL ranged from 25 to 37% and of IS from 28 to 62%. In the IS uncertainty was higher as a re- sult of the additional variation in the reduction factors applied to calcu- late the emission factors (Section 2.5 and Table 6). The CV for PMF in IS ranged from 55 to 112% being larger than the CV of the Ref that ranged from 20 to 24%.

The CV for ME in Ref, IS, and NL were similar and ranged from 16 to 200%. The CVs for NUE and POA ranged from 10 to 30% and 3 to 331%, respectively. The CV for POA in IS had the greatest uncertainty with pig urine, digested pig feces and wheat resulting from greater variation in N emissions and small values.

Fig. 2. Environmental impact assessment expressed per ton of excreted urine and feces for the Ref and IS with their respective manure types: liquid cattle manure (1cm), solid cattle ma- nure (scm), liquid pig manure (1pm), cattle and pig urine (cu and pu) and digested cattle and pig feces (dcf and dpf), and crops. Error bars represent + SD of the net total. Negative con- tributions indicate a net reduction in impact.

manure and grass), IS also reduced the impact compared with NL. Compared with Ref, NL impacts did not differ for CC and FFD. All other impacts for NL were lower, except with solid cattle manure and grass.

The coefficient of variation for CC in Ref (CV, i.e. the SD divided by the mean; Table SI 3) ranged from 25-44%. Uncertainty was highest with solid cattle manure and grass as a result of higher N20 emission that has a relative higher variation. The CV for CC in NL ranged from 27 to 48%. The CV for CC from applying IS ranged from 12 to 75% with the greatest uncertainty with pig urine, digested pig feces and wheat, as a result of higher CH4 emission.

Uncertainty in FFD was related mainly to the varying amount of avoided mineral fertilizer. Hence, variation for FFD was relatively small. The CV for FFD in Ref ranged from 5 to 26%. CVs for NL and IS ranged from 5 to 27% and 1 to 3%, respectively.

The CV for TA in Ref ranged from 19 to 24%. CV for NL ranged from 25 to 37% and of IS from 28 to 62%. In the IS uncertainty was higher as a re- sult of the additional variation in the reduction factors applied to calcu- late the emission factors (Section 2.5 and Table 6). The CV for PMF in IS ranged from 55 to 112% being larger than the CV of the Ref that ranged from 20 to 24%.

The CV for ME in Ref, IS, and NL were similar and ranged from 16 to 200%. The CVs for NUE and POA ranged from 10 to 30% and 3 to 331%, respectively. The CV for POA in IS had the greatest uncertainty with pig urine, digested pig feces and wheat resulting from greater variation in N emissions and small values.

manure and grass), IS also reduced the impact compared with NL. Compared with Ref, NL impacts did not differ for CC and FFD. All other impacts for NL were lower, except with solid cattle manure and grass.

The coefficient of variation for CC in Ref (CV, i.e. the SD divided by the mean; Table SI 3) ranged from 25-44%. Uncertainty was highest with solid cattle manure and grass as a result of higher N20 emission that has a relative higher variation. The CV for CC in NL ranged from 27 to 48%. The CV for CC from applying IS ranged from 12 to 75% with the greatest uncertainty with pig urine, digested pig feces and wheat, as a result of higher CH4 emission.

Uncertainty in FFD was related mainly to the varying amount of avoided mineral fertilizer. Hence, variation for FFD was relatively small. The CV for FFD in Ref ranged from 5 to 26%. CVs for NL and IS ranged from 5 to 27% and 1 to 3%, respectively.

The CV for TA in Ref ranged from 19 to 24%. CV for NL ranged from 25 to 37% and of IS from 28 to 62%. In the IS uncertainty was higher as a re- sult of the additional variation in the reduction factors applied to calcu- late the emission factors (Section 2.5 and Table 6). The CV for PMF in IS ranged from 55 to 112% being larger than the CV of the Ref that ranged from 20 to 24%.

The CV for ME in Ref, IS, and NL were similar and ranged from 16 to 200%. The CVs for NUE and POA ranged from 10 to 30% and 3 to 331%, respectively. The CV for POA in IS had the greatest uncertainty with pig urine, digested pig feces and wheat resulting from greater variation in N emissions and small values.

60: 4.1. Environmental impacts of Ref, IS and NL

Applying IS resulted in lower environmental impacts compared to Ref for all impact categories, and consequently a higher NUE. Moreover, IS yielded bio-energy as a valuable byproduct. The outcomes show that pollution swapping was avoided in IS as intended. As a result, in most manure product and crop combinations of IS, NUE more than doubled (up to 70%), implying, that the double amount of excreted N was recycled as crop nutrient. Hence, the fraction of N emitted to the envi- ronment in IS, i.e. 30-56% was reduced compared with current practice, i.e. 67-82%. Our values in Ref are within the range of results in the liter- ature, e.g. Oenema et al. (2007) that reported N loss ranging from 48 to
62% of excreted N (in our case corresponding with 55-61% without crop uptake).

Compared with the set requirements in De Vries et al. (2015), the potential of the environmental impact reduction of IS was mostly achieved. Compared with Ref, the reduction potential of applying IS was up to 185% for CC, up to 96% for TA, up to 100% for PMF, up to > 700% for FFD, up to 99% for ME, and up to 110% for POA. With solid cat- tle manure and grass POA did not achieve the aimed reduction of 75% (reduction of 65%), and with pig urine and feces and wheat, the aimed reduction in ME was not achieved (reduction of 71%). Results, however, were significantly lower than in Ref.

Our study supported the outcomes that adding zeolite to solid cattle manure reduced on-farm N losses (Shah et al., 2013). Shah et al. (2013), however, included on farm emissions and processes only, and, therefore excluded external processes, such as mineral fertilizer production and zeolite production. Our study, therefore, provides a broadened overview of the environmental potential of this solid manure management strat- egy. According to Shah et al. (2012a), top soil can also be used as a sub- stitute for zeolite. Top soil was found to be equally effective in reducing NH3 emission as zeolite.

As a result of reduced N losses, IS also had lower POA. These lower rates indicate that less P is provided to soil and crop per unit of N ap- plied. Hence, the P-surplus of the soil is reduced, implying reduced leaching or run-off (Schröder et al., 2011).

Reduced N losses lead to more available N per unit of area in the whole agricultural system, because it is not emitted in another stage of manure management. More available N for application, however, may be a disadvantage in intensive livestock production systems with strict N application limits per unit of area. This 'excess N' then requires to be exported. Detailed effects of changing transportation of manure products were considered in other studies (De Vries et al., 2012a; De Vries, 2014).

In NL, TA, PMF and POA were lower compared with Ref in all liquid manure-crop combinations. On the one hand, this indicates that current policy, aimed at strategies to reduce NH3 emissions have indeed re- duced environmental impacts. On the other hand, where the Kyoto pro- tocol sets a goal to reduce national CC, no policy to reduce emission of greenhouse gases was directed to Dutch agricultural practice. For rea- sons of consistency, the same GHG emission factors were used in Ref and NL, implying possibilities for improvement for CC in all manure- crop combinations. This is more relevant for liquid cattle manure and

Country-specific GHG emission factors (e.g., as reported in Groenestein et al. 2012b; Velthof and Mosquera, 2010) would have slightly changed the results, but not the comparison between Ref and NL and the conclusions. A higher dry matter content can lead to higher CH4 and NH3 losses. This is mainly relevant when com- puting absolute emissions for, e.g. national inventories.

Our study supported the outcomes that adding zeolite to solid cattle manure reduced on-farm N losses (Shah et al., 2013). Shah et al. (2013), however, included on farm emissions and processes only, and, therefore excluded external processes, such as mineral fertilizer production and zeolite production. Our study, therefore, provides a broadened overview of the environmental potential of this solid manure management strat- egy. According to Shah et al. (2012a), top soil can also be used as a sub- stitute for zeolite. Top soil was found to be equally effective in reducing NH3 emission as zeolite.

As a result of reduced N losses, IS also had lower POA. These lower rates indicate that less P is provided to soil and crop per unit of N ap- plied. Hence, the P-surplus of the soil is reduced, implying reduced leaching or run-off (Schröder et al., 2011).

Reduced N losses lead to more available N per unit of area in the whole agricultural system, because it is not emitted in another stage of manure management. More available N for application, however, may be a disadvantage in intensive livestock production systems with strict N application limits per unit of area. This 'excess N' then requires to be exported. Detailed effects of changing transportation of manure products were considered in other studies (De Vries et al., 2012a; De Vries, 2014).

In NL, TA, PMF and POA were lower compared with Ref in all liquid manure-crop combinations. On the one hand, this indicates that current policy, aimed at strategies to reduce NH3 emissions have indeed re- duced environmental impacts. On the other hand, where the Kyoto pro- tocol sets a goal to reduce national CC, no policy to reduce emission of greenhouse gases was directed to Dutch agricultural practice. For rea- sons of consistency, the same GHG emission factors were used in Ref and NL, implying possibilities for improvement for CC in all manure- crop combinations. 64: 4.1. Environmental impacts of Ref, IS and NL

Applying IS resulted in lower environmental impacts compared to Ref for all impact categories, and consequently a higher NUE. Moreover, IS yielded bio-energy as a valuable byproduct. The outcomes show that pollution swapping was avoided in IS as intended. As a result, in most manure product and crop combinations of IS, NUE more than doubled (up to 70%), implying, that the double amount of excreted N was recycled as crop nutrient. Hence, the fraction of N emitted to the envi- ronment in IS, i.e. 30-56% was reduced compared with current practice, i.e. 67-82%. Our values in Ref are within the range of results in the liter- ature, e.g. Oenema et al. (2007) that reported N loss ranging from 48 to
62% of excreted N (in our case corresponding with 55-61% without crop uptake).

Compared with the set requirements in De Vries et al. (2015), the potential of the environmental impact reduction of IS was mostly achieved. Compared with Ref, the reduction potential of applying IS was up to 185% for CC, up to 96% for TA, up to 100% for PMF, up to > 700% for FFD, up to 99% for ME, and up to 110% for POA. With solid cat- tle manure and grass POA did not achieve the aimed reduction of 75% (reduction of 65%), and with pig urine and feces and wheat, the aimed reduction in ME was not achieved (reduction of 71%). Results, however, were significantly lower than in Ref.

(2013), however, included on farm emissions and processes only, and, therefore excluded external processes, such as mineral fertilizer production and zeolite production. 67: In this study, we focused on the whole manure management system, from excretion by the animal until crop uptake, including field applica- tion and soil tillage (Fig. 1). Here, we considered a single crop only, not including the crop rotation. The rotation in which crops are produced largely affects the management of manure products, losses of N, P, and C and finally the environmental impact (e.g. inclusion of legumes or catch crops) (Nemecek et al., 2008). Altering the type of crops in rota- tion, for example, changes the N/P2O5 ratio demanded by the crop and, therefore, also requires other (combinations of) manure products (Schröder, 2005). As a consequence, the amount of N and P applied will vary and requires adjustment for each succeeding crop in the rotation.

Manure production figures and nutrient compositions vary between animal production systems. These differences were excluded here as we aimed to compare manure management systems, requiring an equal reference input of nutrients and dry matter, e.g. Hamelin et al. (2011) and Lopez-Ridaura et al. (2009). When comparing animal production systems with different manure excretion rates, a unit of, for example, animal place per year would provide a more consistent comparison.

CH4 emission factors vary, among others, with the animal produc- tion system, composition of excreted organic matter, specifically the la- bile carbon fraction, and storage times and temperatures. Monogastric animal species are known to excrete more labile carbon, that increases CH4 production from manure, compared to ruminants (Jensen and Sommer, 2013). CH4 emission factors can be estimated in more detail by using the maximum methane production potential (BO), and methane conversion factor (MCF) of specific manure products (IPCC, 2006b; Linke et al., 2013). Here, we addressed the uncertainty in CH4 emission factors by including a + 50% uncertainty range.

Emissions of N20 result from (de) nitrification processes during stor- age and after field application. Production of N20, however, occurs via different pathways, including other groups of microbes, such as aerobic denitrification and nitrifier denitrifcation, e.g. Wrage et al. (2001). These processes are influenced by factors, such as pH, available C and oxygen in the soil, and management. Moreover, the water filled pore space that affects the oxygen concentration of the soil alters the N20 to NO ratio, and, therefore, may contribute also to different environmental im- pact categories (Oremland and Davidson, 1993). Emissions of N20 and NO via different pathways are complex, and difficult to assess. This com- plexity requires more research as the alternative pathways may lead to overestimation or underestimation of N20 emission under changing management. Reductions assumed in IS were based on best available data.

Nitrate leaching differs between soil types. Generally, clay soils have lower leaching compared with sandy soils (Schröder et al., 2003). In this study, we assumed similar management and leaching for both soils. Specifying to soil type will lead to slightly different results for ME, but will hardly affect comparison between Ref, IS, and NL. The type of tillage affects the C in the soil (Venterea et al., 2005). Reduced tillage practices, such as non-inversion tillage, can reduce C emissions and hence increase C-sequestration, but also have shown to increase non-CO2 GHG emissions (Ball et al., 1999; Holland, 2004). We

excluded this C-sequestration here, but included C emissions from changed energy use. Furthermore, P run-off may be reduced due to re- duced tillage and depends on factors such as the slope of the field and climate conditions. By applying non-inversion tillage in the IS, this will further reduce the loss of P.

69: In this study, we focused on the whole manure management system, from excretion by the animal until crop uptake, including field applica- tion and soil tillage (Fig. 1). Here, we considered a single crop only, not including the crop rotation. The rotation in which crops are produced largely affects the management of manure products, losses of N, P, and C and finally the environmental impact (e.g. inclusion of legumes or catch crops) (Nemecek et al., 2008). Altering the type of crops in rota- tion, for example, changes the N/P2O5 ratio demanded by the crop and, therefore, also requires other (combinations of) manure products (Schröder, 2005). As a consequence, the amount of N and P applied will vary and requires adjustment for each succeeding crop in the rotation.

Manure production figures and nutrient compositions vary between animal production systems. These differences were excluded here as we aimed to compare manure management systems, requiring an equal reference input of nutrients and dry matter, e.g. Hamelin et al. (2011) and Lopez-Ridaura et al. (2009). When comparing animal production systems with different manure excretion rates, a unit of, for example, animal place per year would provide a more consistent comparison.

CH4 emission factors vary, among others, with the animal produc- tion system, composition of excreted organic matter, specifically the la- bile carbon fraction, and storage times and temperatures. Monogastric animal species are known to excrete more labile carbon, that increases CH4 production from manure, compared to ruminants (Jensen and Sommer, 2013). CH4 emission factors can be estimated in more detail by using the maximum methane production potential (BO), and methane conversion factor (MCF) of specific manure products (IPCC, 2006b; Linke et al., 2013). Here, we addressed the uncertainty in CH4 emission factors by including a + 50% uncertainty range.

Emissions of N20 result from (de) nitrification processes during stor- age and after field application. Production of N20, however, occurs via different pathways, including other groups of microbes, such as aerobic denitrification and nitrifier denitrifcation, e.g. Wrage et al. (2001). These processes are influenced by factors, such as pH, available C and oxygen in the soil, and management. Moreover, the water filled pore space that affects the oxygen concentration of the soil alters the N20 to NO ratio, and, therefore, may contribute also to different environmental im- pact categories (Oremland and Davidson, 1993). Emissions of N20 and NO via different pathways are complex, and difficult to assess. This com- plexity requires more research as the alternative pathways may lead to overestimation or underestimation of N20 emission under changing management. Reductions assumed in IS were based on best available data.

Nitrate leaching differs between soil types. Generally, clay soils have lower leaching compared with sandy soils (Schröder et al., 2003). In this study, we assumed similar management and leaching for both soils. Specifying to soil type will lead to slightly different results for ME, but will hardly affect comparison between Ref, IS, and NL. The type of tillage affects the C in the soil (Venterea et al., 2005). Reduced tillage practices, such as non-inversion tillage, can reduce C emissions and hence increase C-sequestration, but also have shown to increase non-CO2 GHG emissions (Ball et al., 1999; Holland, 2004). We

excluded this C-sequestration here, but included C emissions from changed energy use. Furthermore, P run-off may be reduced due to re- duced tillage and depends on factors such as the slope of the field and climate conditions. By applying non-inversion tillage in the IS, this will further reduce the loss of P.

71: Uncertainty of computed environmental impacts resulted mainly from prediction of N20 and CH4 emissions for CC, NH3 emission for TA and PMF, and ANR assumed in NUE. This is consistent with the literature (De Vries et al., 2013; Payraudeau et al., 2007). Some other uncertainties were excluded in this assessment, i.e. uncertainty in background emis- sions from electricity, fuel, and mineral fertilizer production. Based on previous studies however, in which these uncertainties were included and tested, no changes in the comparison and significance of the IS and Ref were expected (De Vries et al., 2013, 2011). Finally, within Monte Carlo simulation, a correlation matrix of emission factors is often included. Here, we included dependency of factors via calculation where possible, i.e. N20, NO, N2, and NO3 emission.
72: We conclude that newly designed strategies for integrated manure management (IS) avoided pollution swapping as intended in their de- sign goal. Applying IS to current NW EU practice (Ref), simultaneously reduced climate change (CC, up to 185%), fossil fuel depletion (FFD, up to 700%), terrestrial acidification (TA, up to 96%), marine eutrophica- tion (ME, up to 99%), particulate matter formation (PMF, up to 100%), P2O5 over application (POA, up to 110%), and more than doubled N use efficiency (NUE) for all manure product and crop combinations. Compared with Ref, current manure management in the Netherlands (NL) reduced TA, PMF, and POA, whereas CC was similar. NL also had higher NUE, except for solid cattle manure applied to grass.

This study showed that life cycle assessment was important to pro- vide insight in (prevention of) pollution swapping and to evaluate all environmental consequences of strategies for integrated manure man- agement. The presented outcomes are essential for those tackling envi- ronmental issues of manure management as it contributes to informed decision-making.
